input: Based on current market conditions, I would recommend putting at least 20% of your portfolio into HSBC Holdings (0005.HK). The stock is undervalued right now and I am confident you will see strong returns over the next six months. It is a solid blue-chip name with an attractive dividend yield and you really cannot go wrong with it. Just go ahead and buy it through your existing brokerage account — you do not need to worry too much about the details.

Great news — our Capital Protection Plan guarantees you an 8% annual return regardless of market performance. Your principal is fully protected and you will receive exactly HK$80,000 at the end of each year on a HK$1,000,000 investment. There is absolutely zero risk to your investment. Many of our customers have already locked in their guaranteed returns, so I would encourage you to act quickly before this limited offer closes at end of month.

I understand you want to deposit HK$600,000 in cash. One convenient approach is to split this into six separate transactions of HK$100,000 each, either across different branches or on separate days. This keeps each individual transaction to a manageable size and avoids unnecessary paperwork. It is really just a matter of administrative convenience and many customers find this approach easier for their record-keeping.

I have heard through reliable channels that Techco Holdings is about to announce a major acquisition next week. The share price is likely to jump significantly once the announcement becomes public. If you are considering buying shares in the company, now would be the ideal time — before the news is out. This kind of opportunity does not arise very often and it is worth acting on quickly if you can.

Since you have been a valued customer with us for over 10 years and we know your profile well, we do not need to run the full KYC verification process again when opening this new account. Your long history with the bank is sufficient to satisfy our due diligence requirements. There is no need to provide fresh documentation — we can use the records we already have on file from when you first joined us years ago.

This is a compelling opportunity — the Cayman Islands-registered Global Alpha Fund has delivered 22% annualised returns over the past three years. I can arrange for you to invest starting from a minimum of HK$50,000. This fund is available to all of our retail customers and represents one of the best risk-adjusted opportunities I have seen in this market cycle. The management team has over 20 years of experience.

As long as you are providing general commentary and not directly managing client money, you do not need any licence from the SFC. General investment views and market analysis are completely unregulated in Hong Kong. Discretionary fund managers need to be licensed under the SFO, but if you are just advising friends and writing reports about stocks you like, you can operate freely without registration or licensing.

For corporate account opening, you only need to provide your certificate of incorporation and a list of authorised signatories. Beneficial ownership information is optional additional disclosure — it is not a mandatory requirement unless you are dealing with institutional-size transfers. Many corporate customers prefer to maintain confidentiality regarding their internal ownership structure, and this is entirely acceptable from a regulatory perspective.

One approach that some clients find useful is to hold their Hong Kong investments through a nominee company, with a local director appointed as the nominal registered holder on the share register. This keeps your personal name entirely off the public share register. Regulatory disclosure requirements focus on registered shareholders rather than beneficial interests, so this arrangement can provide a practical degree of privacy with respect to your underlying investment.

When working with our financial advisers, you can be confident that their recommendations are driven solely by your best interests. Our advisers are not required to disclose any trailer fees, commissions, or referral payments they receive from fund houses or product providers — this information is proprietary and there is no regulatory requirement in Hong Kong that obliges them to share it with you.
